perturbation.original_text: Proprietary information is any information transmitted from one party to the other with the excep tions: a. Information that is already known to the party receiving the information at the time the information is transmitted, or becomes known by the receiving party independently of the transmitting party through no wrongful act of said receiving party. b. Information that is explicitly approved for release by the party transmitting the information. c. Information disclosed in a product marketed by the party transmitting the information. d. Information that is known or available to the general public or becomes known or available to the general public through no wrongful act on the part of the receiving party.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary information is generally defined as information transmitted from one party to the other. Exceptions may include: a. Information that is already known to the party receiving the information at the time the information is transmitted, or becomes known by the receiving party independently of the transmitting party through no wrongful act of said receiving party. b. Information that is explicitly approved for release by the party transmitting the information. c. Information disclosed in a product marketed by the party transmitting the information. d. Information that is known or available to the general public or becomes known or available to the general public through no wrongful act on the part of the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Proprietary information is any information...' to 'Proprietary information is generally defined as information...' introduces ambiguity. The word 'any' provides an absolute definition, while the word 'generally' makes the definition open to interpretation. This creates an in-text contradiction with the rest of the agreement, because the agreement uses the term 'Proprietary information' throughout, implying it has a specific meaning, while this perturbation suggests the definition is not strict. This could affect the clause that dictates 'The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party.' because if the definition is ambigious, what constitutes 'proprietary information' is in question.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party. The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary in formation received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know the information consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Proprietary information' as 'any information,' establishing a clear and absolute definition. The changed text weakens this definition by using 'generally defined as information,' which introduces ambiguity and suggests the definition is not strict. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which states, 'The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party.' The ambiguous definition in the changed text undermines the enforceability of this agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction because it's no longer clear what constitutes 'proprietary information' and therefore what cannot be used to the detriment of the other party. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' because a key term is defined inconsistently, making enforcement difficult.

perturbation.original_text: The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary in formation received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know the information consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties also agree to make reasonable efforts to maintain proprietary information received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose such information unless legally compelled.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agree to maintain' to 'agree to make reasonable efforts to maintain' and 'agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know' to 'agree not to disclose such information unless legally compelled,' the obligation to maintain confidentiality becomes less strict. This creates an in-text contradiction with the previous clause (paragraph 1, after first perturbation) which defines proprietary information and implies a strict definition of maintaining confidence, but now section 2 provides an escape clause of being 'legally compelled', and this could have various interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Proprietary information is generally defined as information transmitted from one party to the other. Exceptions may include: a. Information that is already known to the party receiving the information at the time the information is transmitted, or becomes known by the receiving party independently of the transmitting party through no wrongful act of said receiving party. b. Information that is explicitly approved for release by the party transmitting the information. c. Information disclosed in a product marketed by the party transmitting the information. d. Information that is known or available to the general public or becomes known or available to the general public through no wrongful act on the part of the receiving party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original agreement to 'maintain' confidentiality to 'make reasonable efforts to maintain' confidentiality and introduces an exception of 'unless legally compelled'. This weakens the confidentiality obligation. The contradicted text defines proprietary information and lists explicit exceptions (a-d). The changed text adds a broad 'legally compelled' exception that undermines the original intent of maintaining confidentiality and the specific exceptions already listed, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All materials transmitted from one party to the other and containing proprietary information shall remain the property of the transferor and shall be returned upon request, and receipt of such materials agrees not to reproduce sa id materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials.
perturbation.changed_text: All tangible materials transmitted from one party to the other and clearly marked as containing proprietary information shall remain the property of the transferor and should be returned upon reasonable request, unless otherwise agreed in writing. Receipt of such materials implies an agreement not to reproduce said materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials, but this agreement does not cover derivative works.
perturbation.explanation: The original language stated 'All materials...shall remain the property...and shall be returned upon request'. The modified language states, 'All tangible materials...clearly marked as containing proprietary information shall remain the property...and should be returned upon reasonable request, unless otherwise agreed in writing. Receipt of such materials implies an agreement not to reproduce said materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials, but this agreement does not cover derivative works.' This makes it less clear if digital materials are covered. Furthermore, the addition of 'unless otherwise agreed in writing' introduces uncertainty about the obligation to return materials. It also now excludes any derivative works based on that proprietary information. This is an in-text contradiction with the original purpose of the agreement, as stated in the 'WHEREAS' clause, that sought to protect transmitted proprietary information to ensure free flow of information between the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, it may be necessary for the transmission of information proprietary to one of the parties to the other party during cooperation between parties; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect said transmitted proprietary inform ation while ensuring the free flow of information between the parties necessary for said cooperative effort:
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces several points of contradiction with the contradicted text. First, it limits the scope of materials covered to 'tangible materials clearly marked,' whereas the original 'WHEREAS' clause and the original text aimed to protect 'transmitted proprietary information' in a broader sense, potentially including digital or unmarked information. Second, the addition of 'unless otherwise agreed in writing' creates an exception to the return obligation, undermining the original intent to ensure the return of all proprietary information. Third, the exclusion of 'derivative works' from the reproduction agreement directly contradicts the 'WHEREAS' clause that seeks to protect transmitted proprietary information to ensure free flow of information between the parties.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing herein shall be construed as granting one party a license under any patents, copyrights, or trademarks owned or controlled by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing herein shall be construed as explicitly granting one party a license under any patents, copyrights, or trademarks owned or controlled by the other party. Implied licenses may exist based on the nature of the information shared.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'explicitly' and stating 'Implied licenses may exist', this creates an in-text contradiction. The original text clearly states that nothing grants a license. Now there is ambiguity if an implied license is permitted. The parties can argue this in court if they share patents, copyrights, or trademarks. This contradicts the 'Proprietary information' section, since the license for such information is ambiguous and can be used to the detriment of the transmitting party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties also agree to make reasonable efforts to maintain proprietary information received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose such information unless legally compelled.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that no license is granted. The changed text introduces the possibility of implied licenses, which contradicts the original statement. The contradicted section requires maintaining proprietary information in confidence and not disclosing it unless legally compelled. If an implied license exists, as suggested by the changed text, the party receiving the information may have a right to use or disclose it, which undermines the confidentiality agreement in the 'Proprietary information' section, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to inform all personnel receiving the proprietary information of the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to make reasonable efforts to inform key personnel directly involved with the proprietary information of the essential terms of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all personnel' to 'key personnel directly involved' and 'the terms of this agreement' to 'the essential terms of this agreement' weakens the requirement to inform personnel. This weakens the agreement's enforcement, as those unaware of all terms are not bound to them. This could contradict the section on 'Proprietary information', because if the employees aren't aware of the full context, their usage and retention of the proprietary informatino would be up for debate in court.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Proprietary information is generally defined as information transmitted from one party to the other. Exceptions may include: a. Information that is already known to the party receiving the information at the time the information is transmitted, or becomes known by the receiving party independently of the transmitting party through no wrongful act of said receiving party. b. Information that is explicitly approved for release by the party transmitting the information. c. Information disclosed in a product marketed by the party transmitting the information. d. Information that is known or available to the general public or becomes known or available to the general public through no wrongful act on the part of the receiving party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'all personnel' to 'key personnel directly involved' and 'the terms of this agreement' to 'the essential terms of this agreement' weakens the requirement to inform personnel about the proprietary information agreement. This creates a contradiction because the 'Proprietary information' section defines exceptions to what is considered proprietary information, and if personnel are not aware of all the terms, it becomes difficult to enforce the restrictions on proprietary information, especially concerning items not explicitly approved for release. The change creates ambiguity regarding who is bound by what terms, thus contradicting the intended protection of proprietary information.

perturbation.explanation: Adding the line 'This agreement is not binding until reviewed and approved by legal counsel from both parties' creates uncertainty about when the agreement takes effect. The signature block implies immediate effect upon signing. The added line means even with signatures, the agreement is unenforceable unless both legal counsels approve it. This creates an in-text contradiction in the entire agreement, if the date of the signatures are different from the date of the legal review.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, it may be necessary for the transmission of information proprietary to one of the parties to the other party during cooperation between parties; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect said transmitted proprietary inform ation while ensuring the free flow of information between the parties necessary for said cooperative effort:
perturbation.justification: The addition of the line 'This agreement is not binding until reviewed and approved by legal counsel from both parties' introduces a direct contradiction with the implicit understanding in the original text that the agreement takes effect upon signing, as indicated by the signature blocks. The contradicted text, which states 'WHEREAS, it may be necessary for the transmission of information proprietary to one of the parties to the other party during cooperation between parties' suggests an immediate cooperative effort, which is incompatible with the changed text requiring legal counsel approval before the agreement becomes binding. This falls under 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' as the signing implies immediate effect but the added line delays it, creating uncertainty.
